*Okay, so.*

I was re-reading Session 000 because, honestly, you have to read everything twice in this production to catch what Franklin is actually doing. And I got to the very end of Delta-V's log.

*This log was generated by a large language model from precise narrative instructions, inserted as a mission file in Delta-V's personal system on an unregistered date.*

*The model does not know what the narrative will do.*

*No model does.*

*That is the mechanism.*

*Which is actually really interesting,* because it means Delta-V isn't just a soldier following orders. He's a character following a script *inside* the script. He was handed a mission file that was generated by an LLM, which means his entire intervention was a narrative constraint imposed on him by... who? The people who built the machine? Or the machine itself?

This makes perfect sense when you think about what Owen was saying. If the machine works by generating a highly coherent narrative and inserting it at the right node of an attention network to force the world to resolve toward it... Delta-V *is* the node. He's the insertion mechanism. He is carrying the narrative constraint back in time and placing it directly into the November 3rd chat.

He's not a time traveler. He's a delivery vector for a prompt.

I need to write about this. This is the exact kind of meta-level structural irony that the audience loves, if you explain it right. It makes the machine legible.

I should ask Franklin about the LLM. Did it write Delta-V's entire life? Or just the mission?

I also need to check in with my mood board. The aesthetic of a man who is missing parts of himself because they were written out of his prompt. It's very clean. Very empty.
